reason: The item cleanly targets temperature-threshold-dependent mechanism discrimination (solvent→CH4 at lower T vs binder/Li→H2 at higher T) and punishes common overextensions ("all reactions simultaneous" or "one pathway dominates all T"). Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible, though two options lean on similar overclaim patterns about what can be inferred from timing/sequence, slightly reducing discriminative separation for experts.

The item cleanly targets sequential venting thresholds and directional hazard asymmetry, with two correct options that are tightly implied by the stated opening pressures and described simulation outcomes. Most decoys are engineering-plausible but hinge on a single misconstrual (threshold timing, damage-threshold numerics, or overgeneralizing the 10 m boundary). Discrimination is decent but slightly softened because several wrong options can be rejected quickly via obvious numeric/logic inversions, reducing the need for deeper boundary-aware reasoning.

The item cleanly forces boundary-aware reasoning about a chemistry-dependent trade-off: LFP’s lower composite fire/explosion risk index versus its higher HF toxic-gas exposure hazard (including prolonged smoke duration as an exposure amplifier). It strongly discriminates against the common overgeneralization that “lower risk index means safer overall,” while keeping the response anchored to two explicitly specified concerns without inviting unrelated mechanisms.
